Notice Of Petition To Compel Arbitration; ***Court Reporter Fees Paid***
Off Calendar May-13-2021 Denied
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances (via ZOOM videoconference):
Atty. Hunter Pyle, Esq. of Hunter Pyle Law, 425.318.0708, for Plaintiff;
Atty. John Darin, Esq. of Hunter Pyle Law, 425.318.0708, for Plaintiff;
Atty. Bree Ullman, Esq. of the Law Office of Bree Ullman, 425.318.0708, for Plaintiff;
Atty. Kaa Boa Yang, Esq. of Lewis Brisbois Bisgaard & Smith LLP, 415.362.2580, for Defendants.

DEFENDANT EUROMOTORS, INC.'s Petition To Compel Arbitration.

The matter is called. ARGUMENT is held. At the conclusion of argument, the tentative ruling is adopted as follows:

Defendant Euromotors, Inc. dba Mercedes-Benz of San Francisco's petition to compel arbitration is denied. Plaintiffs prove both procedural and substantive unconscionability. (Pinnacle Museum Tower Assn. v. Pinnacle Market Development (US), LLC (2012) 59 Cal.4th 223, 247 [to prove unconscionability, the party resisting arbitration bears the burden of proving both procedural and substantive unconscionability].) Davis v. TWC Dealer Group Inc. (2019) 41 Cal.App.5th 662 is instructive. The agreements containing arbitration provisions and signed by each plaintiff are substantially similar to the arbitration agreements analyzed in Davis. The arbitration agreements at issue here have a high degree of procedural unconscionability. (See Compendium of Arbitration Provisions ISO Opp., sections 1-4; see also Davis v. TWC Dealer Group Inc., 41 Cal.App.5th at 672 [procedural unconscionability is "extraordinarily high" when the terms are written in a single block without paragraphs and when the arbitration clause is visually impenetrable].) The agreements are written in a way "to thwart, rather than promote, understanding." (See OTO, L.L.C. v. Kho (2019) 8 Cal.5th 111, 128-29 [procedural unconscionability found where the agreement is opaque, sentences complex, and filled with statutory references and legal jargon].) Given the high degree of procedural unconscionability, a relatively low degree of substantial unconscionability is enough to render the agreements unenforceable. (Id. at 130.) Like the arbitration agreements in Davis and Kho, the agreements are in small typeface, include broad language that could preclude PAGA actions (public policy violation), and are plainly confusing. (See Davis, 41 Cal.App.5th at 674-676.)

Order signed.
